response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
Look, when things get heated like that—talking about medicine or what’s going on with the virus—it can really mess with your head. It feels like everyone got an opinion, and they all sound so sure of themselves, even if they're shouting it across Facebook or something. Honestly, I find these kinds of conversations draining. My stomach just gets tight thinking about it. You spend time dealing with people who are agitated, arguing over details you don't even know much about, and by the time it’s over, you feel completely worn out. It makes me want to just go home and sit in my garden or lose myself in a game for a while. 

If I had to give some advice, especially if someone keeps trying to drag me into one of these big debates, I reckon the best thing is to just change the subject, plain and simple. You don’t have to prove anything to anybody out there. It's not worth the emotional effort. When people get really intense, it sounds like they aren't actually listening to what you're saying; they're just waiting for a chance to hit back with something strong. Because I'm feeling pretty fragile these days, and my nerves are already shot from dealing with work stress on top of everything else, getting pulled into arguing facts or theories about vaccines just makes me more anxious. 

Maybe the goal shouldn't be to 'win' the discussion or even to convince the other person that they are wrong. Maybe it’s just about protecting your own peace. If a conversation starts spiraling down a rabbit hole of medical claims and conflicting data, you can politely say something like, 'You know what? I think we should talk about something else.' Or, if it's online, just hit the mute button or block them. Seriously, walking away is the strongest move. Focus on the things that actually help you—like spending time with my family, getting out in the fresh air when I'm gardening, or just keeping busy building something at work. Those are real anchors. Don't let other people’s arguments pull you under, okay? Just keep your head down and take care of yourself first.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 49-year-old single woman plumber living in New York with lower secondary education. Key psychological traits include 'depressed' (high neuroticism), low openness, conscientiousness, extraversion, and agreeableness. Her tone should be anxious, withdrawn, defensive of emotional boundaries, practical, somewhat fatalistic about social conflict, and reflective of exhaustion. The advice must prioritize self-care and disengagement from stressful topics. Examples used relate to her hobbies (gardening, video games) and general feelings of anxiety/stress.
